title: Turkish Red Lentil Soup (Mercimek Çorbası)

subs.name: yellow moong dal — same colour, slightly milder flavour
pumpkin, diced — makes the soup sweeter and a deeper orange

ingredients.name: red chilli powder
visual_checks: divided: half for the soup, half for the finishing butter
ingredients.notes: divided: half for the soup, half for the finishing butter

subs.name: 1 tsp vinegar stirred in at the end
subs.notes: a workable substitute when lemon is unavailable

steps.text: Heat 1.5 tablespoons oil in a heavy-bottomed pot on medium flame. Add cumin seeds and let them sizzle for 20 seconds. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent.
Add garlic, carrot and potato. Stir for 2 minutes. Add cumin powder, turmeric and half the chilli powder. Stir for 30 seconds to coat everything in spice.

Rinse the red lentils until the water runs clear. Add them to the pot with 1 litre water and salt. Bring to a boil, skimming any foam, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook for 20–25 minutes until the lentils and vegetables are completely soft.
Remove from heat and blend with a hand blender (or in batches in a mixie) until completely smooth. If the soup is too thick, add 100–200 ml hot water and blend again. Taste for salt.

For the finishing drizzle: heat the remaining half-tablespoon oil in a small pan on medium-high until hot. Remove from flame, add the remaining chilli powder and swirl — it will sizzle. Pour this bright red oil over each bowl.
Serve hot with a squeeze of lemon juice, which brightens the soup considerably. A slice of crusty bread alongside is traditional.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: masoor-dal → yellow moong dal — same colour, slightly milder flavour (cooks in the same time); carrot → pumpkin, diced — makes the soup sweeter and a deeper orange (works very well); lemon → 1 tsp vinegar stirred in at the end (a workable substitute when lemon is unavailable)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Red masoor dal is one of India's most common lentils and is very affordable. Cook in a pressure cooker for 2 whistles instead of simmering — faster and identical result. Blend in your domestic mixie. The chilli butter drizzle is very similar to the tadka technique already used in Indian dal.
